items.question: Do I need any background in spirituality or self-help to join?
items.answer: Not at all. The work doesn't ask you to adopt any belief, tradition, or philosophy. It simply invites you to look — honestly and openly — at how you live. People from all walks of life have walked this path. What matters is your willingness to engage.
items.author: Balaji

items.question: Why is the program free? What's the catch?
items.answer: There is no catch. Joyous Living is an offering — a way of giving back the work that has shaped my own life. Buddha said “enlightenment is a human being’s birthright”. It is not mine to charge all I am to do is share. The only thing I ask in return is your full participation. The program is free in cost, but it asks for something more valuable: your honest engagement.
items.author: Balaji

items.question: How is this different from therapy or coaching?
items.answer: Therapy often focuses on healing the past; coaching often focuses on future goals. Joyous Living is about your relationship with yourself right now — uncovering who you are beneath the patterns, opinions, and roles you've inherited. It complements both, but it's its own distinct practice.
items.author: Balaji

items.question: I'm extremely busy. Can I really commit to 10–12 weeks?
items.answer: Busy is often a symptom of living on autopilot. Two hours a week, for a season, is a small price for a life that no longer feels rushed by default. If you find yourself making the case for why you can't — that itself may be the most important question to bring to the first session.
items.author: Balaji

items.question: Will sessions be in person or online?
items.answer: Both formats are offered depending on the cohort. Once you register, I'll share the schedule and format options that fit your timezone and availability.
items.author: Balaji
